humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs using multiple-choice questions from humanities subjects (such as history, philosophy, and world religions) while explicitly identifying and analyzing the impact of Western-centric cultural and geographical knowledge on multilingual model performance.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Textual multiple-choice question answering across 42 languages, combining human translations, post-edits, and machine-translated content.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates factual knowledge and reasoning across multiple disciplines, specifically dividing tasks into culturally-sensitive (requiring localized cultural, geographic, or dialectal context) and culturally-agnostic subsets.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Spans 57 diverse subjects across STEM, Humanities, Social Sciences, Business, and Medicine.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates several SOTA open and proprietary LLMs including GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, Aya Expanse, Command R/R+, Gemma 2, Mistral Nemo, and SEA-LION v3.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Evaluates subjects within History, Philosophy, Jurisprudence, World Religions, and Dialect/Language variations.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates performance using accuracy, relative rank changes, and position shifts.

published_after_march_2023.note: The preprint was updated and released in February 2025.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the benchmark evaluates English alongside 41 other languages.

summary: Global-MMLU (GMMLU) is a multilingual evaluation benchmark across 42 languages designed to analyze and address linguistic and cultural biases in machine-translated versions of the MMLU dataset. The authors systematically annotate MMLU to identify culturally-sensitive and culturally-agnostic subsets, exposing a heavy Western-centric bias in the original questions, and release a high-quality human-verified translation to enable more accurate global evaluations.
key_findings: The analysis reveals that 28% of MMLU questions require culturally-sensitive (CS) knowledge, with 86.5% of those being Western-centric and 84.9% of geographic questions focusing on North America or Europe. Evaluating models on CS vs. culturally-agnostic (CA) subsets causes significant ranking volatility (averaging 5.7 rank changes on CS vs. 3.4 on CA), showing that standard translated MMLU distorts true multilingual capabilities.
